class="w-full h-full object-cover object-top"></div><article class="min-w-0 px-1"><h2 class="text-xl font-semibold text-foreground mt-10 mb-4 first:mt-0">Use 21st in your agent</h2>
<p class="text-[15px] text-foreground/80 mb-4 leading-[1.75]">21st is not just a place you browse. It is a set of tools your coding agent can use directly: search the catalog, install components, generate UI, and publish your own work, all from the terminal or any MCP client. This release makes connecting it a one-click, one-command affair, and rebuilds the CLI underneath so every surface behaves the same.</p>
<h2 class="text-xl font-semibold text-foreground mt-10 mb-4 first:mt-0">One endpoint, every client</h2>
<p class="text-[15px] text-foreground/80 mb-4 leading-[1.75]">Everything an agent does with 21st runs through a single endpoint: <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">https://21st.dev/api/mcp</code>. The new <strong class="font-semibold text-foreground">Use 21st in your agent</strong> page gives you a tab per client, each with the exact snippet or deep link that editor expects:</p>
<ul class="list-disc list-outside pl-5 space-y-1.5 mb-4 text-[15px] text-foreground/80">
<li class="pl-0.5 leading-[1.75]"><strong class="font-semibold text-foreground">Claude Code</strong> registers the server with one command, or writes the config for you.</li>
<li class="pl-0.5 leading-[1.75]"><strong class="font-semibold text-foreground">Claude Desktop</strong> connects through the OAuth connector flow, no key to paste.</li>
<li class="pl-0.5 leading-[1.75]"><strong class="font-semibold text-foreground">Cursor</strong> has a one-click install deep link.</li>
<li class="pl-0.5 leading-[1.75]"><strong class="font-semibold text-foreground">Codex</strong> gets a <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">~/.codex/config.toml</code> entry with a bearer-token env var.</li>
<li class="pl-0.5 leading-[1.75]"><strong class="font-semibold text-foreground">Lovable</strong> connects over OAuth.</li>
<li class="pl-0.5 leading-[1.75]"><strong class="font-semibold text-foreground">Any other MCP client</strong> gets a generic HTTP + <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">x-api-key</code> config.</li>
</ul>
<p class="text-[15px] text-foreground/80 mb-4 leading-[1.75]">There is a "Copy as prompt" button on every tab, so you can hand the whole setup to an agent and let it wire itself up. The snippets mask the middle of your API key on screen so you can share your screen safely, while the copy buttons still carry the real key.</p>
<h2 class="text-xl font-semibold text-foreground mt-10 mb-4 first:mt-0">The 21st CLI, rebuilt</h2>
<p class="text-[15px] text-foreground/80 mb-4 leading-[1.75]">Under all of this is a rebuilt command-line tool, <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">@21st-dev/cli</code>, that unifies what used to be scattered across separate tools. One binary now covers the whole workflow:</p>

py-0.5 font-mono">iterate</code>, <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">take</code>), publishing (<code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">publish</code>, <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 <h3 class="text-base font-semibold text-foreground mt-7 mb-3">What <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">init --client</code> actually does</h3>
<p class="text-[15px] text-foreground/80 mb-4 leading-[1.75]"><code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">init</code> is the piece that removes the config busywork. Point it at your editor and it writes the right file in the right format:</p>
<ul class="list-disc list-outside pl-5 space-y-1.5 mb-4 text-[15px] text-foreground/80">
<li class="pl-0.5 leading-[1.75]"><code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">--client claude</code> writes <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">.mcp.json</code> in your project.</li>
<li class="pl-0.5 leading-[1.75]"><code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">--client cursor</code> writes <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">~/.cursor/mcp.json</code>.</li>
<li class="pl-0.5 leading-[1.75]"><code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">--client codex</code> prints the TOML block for <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">~/.codex/config.toml</code>.</li>
</ul>
<p class="text-[15px] text-foreground/80 mb-4 leading-[1.75]">Crucially, it <strong class="font-semibold text-foreground">merges</strong> into your existing MCP config with a JSON patch instead of overwriting it, so your other servers stay put. In CI or scripts it drops an <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">${API_KEY_21ST}</code> env reference instead of baking a secret into a committed file.</p>
<h2 class="text-xl font-semibold text-foreground mt-10 mb-4 first:mt-0">Same tools for you and your agent</h2>
<p class="text-[15px] text-foreground/80 mb-4 leading-[1.75]">The reason all of this stays consistent is that the CLI, the MCP endpoint, and the website talk to the same core client and the same server. Your terminal and your agent see identical search results, the same rate limits, and the same catalog. There is no "CLI version" of the tools that drifts from the "agent version"; there is one implementation.</p>
<p class="text-[15px] text-foreground/80 mb-4 leading-[1.75]">Auth is unified the same way. Every surface authenticates with a <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">21st_sk_</code> key, and the server accepts three credential paths behind the scenes: an API key header for scripts and CI, a CLI session token from <code class="bg-foreground/[0.08] text-foreground/80 text-[0.85em] rounded px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono">21st login</code>, or an OAuth token for connector-style clients like Claude Desktop. Whichever door you come through, you land on the same tools.</p>
<p class="text-[15px] text-foreground/80 mb-4 leading-[1.75]">That is what makes agent workflows real: your assistant can search 21st, install a component, generate a new one, and even publish it back, using the same key and the same endpoint you use by hand.</p>
